Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across portions of southeast Minnesota on the morning of January 7th. The dangerous wind chills occurred behind an area of low pressure that moved across the central Mississippi River Valley on January 6th and as a strong area of high pressure dropped south out of Canada onto the High Plains. These two features combined to push air temperatures down into the single digits and teens below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 42 below from a mesonet reporting station near Grand Meadow (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Fillmore County on the morning of January 7th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 35 below from a mesonet reporting station near Canton.

In the wake of the large thunderstorm complex on Monday, June 22nd, strong winds developed causing more damage to portions of southern Minnesota. These winds were created from the decaying thunderstorm complex, and were measured at several places up to 68 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. James airport AWOS measured a wind gust of 59 knots. In addition, a Minnesota Department of Transportation traffic and weather sensor just west of the Watonwan, Cottonwood county line, had a measured wind gust of 73 mph at 7:03 am LST. This sensor also reported sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph for over a half hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were strong winds over northeast Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin on April 2nd, with widespread maximum wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. There were a couple measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, including at the Duluth airport and near Breezy Point.  A maximum wind gust speed of 71 mph was measured on the Blatnik Bridge between Duluth and Superior, WI.  This was not included in the events since this measurement is taken very high up on the bridge.  There were scattered reports of trees down across northeast Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin.  The trees may have been in poor condition and more susceptible to the wind.  A woman was struck and badly injured by a falling tree while she was riding her ATV near Aurora, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a maximum measured wind gust at the ASOS at the Duluth International Airport (KDLH), which is 5 miles northwest of Duluth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds, directly and indirectly associated with thunderstorms, were reported at several locations in southwest Minnesota near and a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. The damaging winds not directly with storms had spread away from storms some distance to the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds not directly associated with thunderstorms produced a measured gust to 60 mph at Luverne Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds at a few locations in southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail which caused a 3 mile wide swath of nearly total crop damage. The amount of crop damage was not known, but the USDA office reported that soybean crops were affected more than corn, and several bean fields were later replanted. Vehicles were also damaged by hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning and early afternoon of May 17th, west central Minnesota was ahead of a vertically stacked low pressure system, which was located over northeast South Dakota. Winds above the surface layer were fairly strong. A few showers formed, which helped to mix these stronger winds down to the surface in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tin roofing panels and attached pieces of board and insulation were torn from an old widespan poultry barn, with pieces of debris knocking down power lines and blowing onto highway80. The old barn was being used for storage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of high based showers and thunderstorms spread into portions of west central Minnesota during the evening of May 2. These showers and thunderstorms only produced about a tenth of an inch of rain, but they packed a punch with strong gusty winds. There were also several dry and wet microbursts, which produced some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large jack pines were blown down across a fence. One of the trees also fell onto the roof of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed west of St. Cloud and moved northeast across the city of Milaca. This storm produced damaging winds which caused several power lines and a large tree to be blown down east of the town along Highway 23.

Damaging winds, directly and indirectly associated with thunderstorms, were reported at several locations in southwest Minnesota near and a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. The damaging winds not directly with storms had spread away from storms some distance to the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds not directly associated with thunderstorms produced a measured gust to 63 mph at Worthington Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of severe thunderstorms moved southward across east central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening hours of Monday, June 29th. There were also several hail swaths that affected portions of the northwest, and western suburbs of the Twin Cities metro area based on the SHAVE project, trained spotters and the general public. ||Specifically, one complex of severe storms developed during the early-mid afternoon hours near the Elk River, and drifted southward producing large hail from Elk River, to Rogers, Maple Grove, Plymouth and Eden Prairie. ||The second wave of severe thunderstorms moved across far east central Minnesota and again produced large area from near Rush City, North Branch, south across the heart of the Twin Cities metro area before dissipating around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of large hail occurred near in the intersection of Highway 62 and Interstate 494, southward around Eden Prairie Center and the junction of 494 and 169.  The largest hail stone was quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Saturday, May 16th, a warm front surged northward from the Minnesota, Iowa border to west central and east central Minnesota. Surface winds backed from the northeast, to the east-southeast once the front went through the area. In addition, there was a sharp clearing line south of this front that led to strong surface heating once the front passed. ||Thunderstorms began to develop across southwest and west central Minnesota during the afternoon, and moved northward. Once the storms moved northward near the warm front, the storms quickly became tornadic. Some tornadoes occurred for only a minute or two, but others had a longer duration of 5 to 10 minutes and looked quite large according to video.  Just about all the tornadoes moved over farm fields that were still open, as corn and bean plants were just beginning to emerge.  A few outbuildings were damaged or destroyed along with roof and tree damage. One of the storms that developed southeast of Litchfield, Minnesota, caused damage to several buildings. Another storm moved near Kimball, Minnesota, and damaged a schools athletic field.

Several thunderstorms developed across the western Twin Cities metro, into east central Minnesota, between 300 and 330 PM LST. One storm developed over the National Weather Service Office in Chanhassen, MN and produced large hail. There was also siding damage to homes just south of the National Weather Service Office in Chanhassen, which reported up to two inches in diameter for hail size. As this storm moved off across the southern part of the Twin Cities area, most of the hail was measured up to the size of nickels.||Another severe thunderstorm developed in the northwest part of the Twin Cities, near Brooklyn Park, where windows were blown out, and then began to move rapidly eastward across the northern and eastern Twin Cities area. Numerous reports of large hail (up to 1.25) and damaging winds were reported along the storm path from near Crystal, Robbinsdale, and Golden Valley, eastward to North Minneapolis, Columbia Heights, Saint Anthony and Arden Hills. Another swath of large hail (up to golf ball size) occurred from near White Bear Lake, eastward to Stillwater. Some of the hail was measured up to a foot deep in some areas. These storms moved quickly eastward into west central Wisconsin and produced more severe hail and damaging winds. ||A secondary line of thunderstorms developed along the Minnesota, Iowa border and produced damaging winds near Albert Lea where power lines were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eight inch diameter tree branch fell across a driveway.

During the early morning hours of Monday, June 22nd, a complex of thunderstorms (classified by some as a weak derecho event) developed across western South Dakota and moved rapidly eastward into southwest Minnesota before daybreak. Several bow-echo segments and a few isolated storms ahead of the main thunderstorm complex affected a large area along and south of the Minnesota River Valley, and in the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities between 4 am and 6 am LST. ||Initially, a line of thunderstorms that bowed out in far southern Minnesota, caused numerous downed trees, power lines, and some structural damage to outbuildings and homes as winds of 60 to 70 mph moved across the area between 4 am and 6 am LST.   ||There were isolated severe storms that developed ahead of the main line and moved northeast across south central and into the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities. Golf ball size hail was reported west of Lakeville before sunrise.

There were also several hail swaths that affected portions of the northwest, and western suburbs of the Twin Cities metro area based on the SHAVE project, trained spotters and the general public. ||Specifically, one complex of severe storms developed during the early-mid afternoon hours near the Elk River, and drifted southward producing large hail from Elk River, to Rogers, Maple Grove, Plymouth and Eden Prairie. ||The second wave of severe thunderstorms moved across far east central Minnesota and again produced large area from near Rush City, North Branch, south across the heart of the Twin Cities metro area before dissipating around sunset.

During the early morning hours of Monday, June 22nd, a complex of thunderstorms (classified by some as a weak derecho event) developed across western South Dakota and moved rapidly eastward into southwest Minnesota before daybreak. Several bow-echo segments and a few isolated storms ahead of the main thunderstorm complex affected a large area along and south of the Minnesota River Valley, and in the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities between 4 am and 6 am LST. ||Initially, a line of thunderstorms that bowed out in far southern Minnesota, caused numerous downed trees, power lines, and some structural damage to outbuildings and homes as winds of 60 to 70 mph moved across the area between 4 am and 6 am LST.   ||There were isolated severe storms that developed ahead of the main line and moved northeast across south central and into the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities. Golf ball size hail was reported west of Lakeville before sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down along with a roof blown off a commercial building near Vernon Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms developed across the western Twin Cities metro, into east central Minnesota, between 300 and 330 PM LST. One storm developed over the National Weather Service Office in Chanhassen, MN and produced large hail. There was also siding damage to homes just south of the National Weather Service Office in Chanhassen, which reported up to two inches in diameter for hail size. As this storm moved off across the southern part of the Twin Cities area, most of the hail was measured up to the size of nickels.||Another severe thunderstorm developed in the northwest part of the Twin Cities, near Brooklyn Park, where windows were blown out, and then began to move rapidly eastward across the northern and eastern Twin Cities area. Numerous reports of large hail (up to 1.25) and damaging winds were reported along the storm path from near Crystal, Robbinsdale, and Golden Valley, eastward to North Minneapolis, Columbia Heights, Saint Anthony and Arden Hills. Another swath of large hail (up to golf ball size) occurred from near White Bear Lake, eastward to Stillwater. Some of the hail was measured up to a foot deep in some areas. These storms moved quickly eastward into west central Wisconsin and produced more severe hail and damaging winds. ||A secondary line of thunderstorms developed along the Minnesota, Iowa border and produced damaging winds near Albert Lea where power lines were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows were blown out near Osseo.

Lightning from thunderstorms on June 28th caused 3 injuries in St. Louis County.  A 51-year-old father and his 4-year-old daughter were struck and injured by lightning around 2 pm near a baseball field in Cherry, MN, which is near the Iron Range.  The father and daughter were watching the baseball game.  The game was delayed because of the thunderstorm, and the father and daughter were struck as they were walking back to their van.  The father suffered a heart attack and was in critical condition.  He was hospitalized for many days. A 12-year-old boy was critically injured after being struck by lightning at Enger Park Golf Course in Duluth.  The boy was a spectator at a tournament being held at the golf course.

The father and daughter were watching the baseball game. The game was delayed because of the thunderstorm, and the father and daughter were struck as they were walking back to their van.

A line of thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved across southeast Minnesota during the early morning of June 22nd. In the Austin (Mower County) area, over three inches of rain fell in about three hours. This rain overwhelmed the drainage systems in the city causing considerable urban flooding to occur. Interstate 90 was closed as parts of it became completely submerged in water and U.S. Highway 218 on the east side of the city also had water on it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 90 in Austin was closed as parts of it became submerged by flood waters. A car was totaled after running into a fire truck on the flooded portion of Interstate 90. U.S. Highway 218 on the east side of Austin also had water on the road and there were numerous reports of street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from thunderstorms on June 28th caused 3 injuries in St. Louis County.  A 51-year-old father and his 4-year-old daughter were struck and injured by lightning around 2 pm near a baseball field in Cherry, MN, which is near the Iron Range.  The father and daughter were watching the baseball game.  The game was delayed because of the thunderstorm, and the father and daughter were struck as they were walking back to their van.  The father suffered a heart attack and was in critical condition.  He was hospitalized for many days. A 12-year-old boy was critically injured after being struck by lightning at Enger Park Golf Course in Duluth.  The boy was a spectator at a tournament being held at the golf course.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12-year-old boy was critically injured after being struck by lightning at Enger Park Golf Course in Duluth, near the fairway, at about 3:50 pm. The boy was a spectator at a tournament being held at the golf course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Monday, June 22nd, a complex of thunderstorms (classified by some as a weak derecho event) developed across western South Dakota and moved rapidly eastward into southwest Minnesota before daybreak. Several bow-echo segments and a few isolated storms ahead of the main thunderstorm complex affected a large area along and south of the Minnesota River Valley, and in the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities between 4 am and 6 am LST. ||Initially, a line of thunderstorms that bowed out in far southern Minnesota, caused numerous downed trees, power lines, and some structural damage to outbuildings and homes as winds of 60 to 70 mph moved across the area between 4 am and 6 am LST.   ||There were isolated severe storms that developed ahead of the main line and moved northeast across south central and into the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities. Golf ball size hail was reported west of Lakeville before sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was uprooted and fell onto a car and building causing significant damage in Sherburn.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down and landed on a house in St. James.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Saturday, May 16th, a warm front surged northward from the Minnesota, Iowa border to west central and east central Minnesota. Surface winds backed from the northeast, to the east-southeast once the front went through the area. In addition, there was a sharp clearing line south of this front that led to strong surface heating once the front passed. ||Thunderstorms began to develop across southwest and west central Minnesota during the afternoon, and moved northward. Once the storms moved northward near the warm front, the storms quickly became tornadic. Some tornadoes occurred for only a minute or two, but others had a longer duration of 5 to 10 minutes and looked quite large according to video.  Just about all the tornadoes moved over farm fields that were still open, as corn and bean plants were just beginning to emerge.  A few outbuildings were damaged or destroyed along with roof and tree damage. One of the storms that developed southeast of Litchfield, Minnesota, caused damage to several buildings. Another storm moved near Kimball, Minnesota, and damaged a schools athletic field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado produced damage at a farm. A 100' x 70' long machine shed was destroyed when it was pushed off its foundation, with metal blown 1.5 miles downwind. Much of the equipment inside the shed was destroyed. A metal fence was blown down, and dozens of trees were broken. The tornado even clipped off some of the new soybeans that had emerged and were only one or two inches out of the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms developed across the western Twin Cities metro, into east central Minnesota, between 300 and 330 PM LST. One storm developed over the National Weather Service Office in Chanhassen, MN and produced large hail. There was also siding damage to homes just south of the National Weather Service Office in Chanhassen, which reported up to two inches in diameter for hail size. As this storm moved off across the southern part of the Twin Cities area, most of the hail was measured up to the size of nickels.||Another severe thunderstorm developed in the northwest part of the Twin Cities, near Brooklyn Park, where windows were blown out, and then began to move rapidly eastward across the northern and eastern Twin Cities area. Numerous reports of large hail (up to 1.25) and damaging winds were reported along the storm path from near Crystal, Robbinsdale, and Golden Valley, eastward to North Minneapolis, Columbia Heights, Saint Anthony and Arden Hills. Another swath of large hail (up to golf ball size) occurred from near White Bear Lake, eastward to Stillwater. Some of the hail was measured up to a foot deep in some areas. These storms moved quickly eastward into west central Wisconsin and produced more severe hail and damaging winds. ||A secondary line of thunderstorms developed along the Minnesota, Iowa border and produced damaging winds near Albert Lea where power lines were blown down.

During the afternoon of Saturday, June 27th, a weak surface low moved southward across eastern North Dakota while a strong disturbance moved southward enhancing the instability and wind shear across western Minnesota. Several severe storms moved southward along the Minnesota, North Dakota border, and produced severe wind gusts, wind damage and large hail. The local emergency manager indicated that the majority of Lac Que Parle county was impacted east of Highway 75 and north of Highway 40, about 7-8 miles wide by 18 miles long.  Townships affected include Agassiz, Perry, Lake Shore North and South, Madison, Hantho and Cerro Gordo.  Wind damage was also reported in Bellingham, Dawson and Boyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local emergency manager received damage reports in areas east of Highway 75, and north of Highway 40 as a severe storm moved through. The following are some of the reports received: one hog barn destroyed, 13 power poles broken, 3 grain bins damaged, one hoop barn damaged, one machine shed damaged, one 5th wheel livestock trailer destroyed, numerous windows broken, and house siding damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Saturday, June 27th, a weak surface low moved southward across eastern North Dakota while a strong disturbance moved southward enhancing the instability and wind shear across western Minnesota. Several severe storms moved southward along the Minnesota, North Dakota border, and produced severe wind gusts, wind damage and large hail. The local emergency manager indicated that the majority of Lac Que Parle county was impacted east of Highway 75 and north of Highway 40, about 7-8 miles wide by 18 miles long.  Townships affected include Agassiz, Perry, Lake Shore North and South, Madison, Hantho and Cerro Gordo.  Wind damage was also reported in Bellingham, Dawson and Boyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of large hail, the largest being ping pong ball size, occurred in an area between the Lac Qui Parle, and Big Stone County line, southeast toward the town of Cerro Gordo.  The combination of hail and wind caused considerable damage to the row crops in this area as well as substantial building/property damage. It was also indicated that an estimated 10,600 acres of soybeans were likely destroyed and another 10,600 acres were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Saturday, May 16th, a warm front surged northward from the Minnesota, Iowa border to west central and east central Minnesota. Surface winds backed from the northeast, to the east-southeast once the front went through the area. In addition, there was a sharp clearing line south of this front that led to strong surface heating once the front passed. ||Thunderstorms began to develop across southwest and west central Minnesota during the afternoon, and moved northward. Once the storms moved northward near the warm front, the storms quickly became tornadic. Some tornadoes occurred for only a minute or two, but others had a longer duration of 5 to 10 minutes and looked quite large according to video.  Just about all the tornadoes moved over farm fields that were still open, as corn and bean plants were just beginning to emerge.  A few outbuildings were damaged or destroyed along with roof and tree damage. One of the storms that developed southeast of Litchfield, Minnesota, caused damage to several buildings. Another storm moved near Kimball, Minnesota, and damaged a schools athletic field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado mostly moved across open fields, and was recorded on video by numerous storm chasers and spotters.  After it touched down in an open field east of Terrace, it lifted off briefly and came back down a couple of times before moving through the western edge of Sedan.  The tornado caused about a dozen trees to be uprooted at a cemetery on the northwest edge of Sedan.  This was a multiple vortex tornado.  It also appears there may have been a satellite tornado that touched down in the back yard of a house on County Road 29, 1/2 mile south of Sedan, but the video is inconclusive as to whether it was a satellite tornado or part of the main tornadic circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of severe thunderstorms moved southward across east central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening hours of Monday, June 29th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of large hail occurred around the city of North Branch. The largest hail stone was tennis ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Monday, June 22nd, a complex of thunderstorms (classified by some as a weak derecho event) developed across western South Dakota and moved rapidly eastward into southwest Minnesota before daybreak. Golf ball size hail was reported west of Lakeville before sunrise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 12th a mid level ridge and hot surface temperatures sat over the Rocky Mountains and high plains, with Minnesota lying on the eastern periphery. By mid afternoon, temperatures had risen into the mid to upper 90s across western and central North Dakota, and into the lower 90s along the Red River Valley. To the east in Minnesota, temperatures peaked in the 80s. Afternoon dew point values rose into the mid 60s to around 70. Thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon over the northern Red River Valley and tracked southeast through Wadena County through the early evening. Then, a second round of storms fired up over Kittson County shortly after the first round exited Wadena County. This second round of storms tracked nearly identically to first round, or from Kittson County southeast through Wadena County. These storms produced very large hail and strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere combined with ample wind shear to result in the development of storms with large hail, severe winds, a tornado, and very heavy rainfall from Alexandria to Lake Mille Lacs...then southeast along Interstate 94. ||Initially, a severe thunderstorm developed near Fergus Falls during the afternoon of Sunday, July 12th.  As the afternoon progressed, several severe thunderstorms developed south toward Wheaton, then spread east-southeast toward Alexandria. One severe storm moved rapidly eastward across the Todd, Wadena county line and eventually produced a tornado and widespread straight-line wind damage. This tornado moved from Wadena County and into Todd County, destroying a barn south of Aldrich and causing numerous downed trees and power lines. The tornado was accompanied by damaging rear flank downdraft and downburst winds, and the same storm produced wind damage in northwest Morrison County. ||Severe storms moved into the area west of Alexandria and produced winds greater than 80 mph in some locations.  Near Brandon, there was structural damage to a large shed, a large pontoon boat was flipped on Maple Lake, and there were numerous downed trees and power lines.   ||Severe storms merged in west central and central Minnesota and then developed more into a line of storms and produced periodic downed trees, power lines, and occasional estimated winds of 60-70 mph as the storms moved southeast along Interstate 94 and into the Twin Cities metro area. Damage was not as widespread after it moved out of central Minnesota and into east central Minnesota, due to less wind shear and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pontoon boat was thrown and flipped over on Maple Lake.

A thunderstorm complex that developed across South Dakota, moved into southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of Saturday, June 20th. There were several reports of large branches and a few trees blown down across Brown County. The town of Comfrey had 6-7 blocks that were closed temporarily due to tree debris across the road. Crop damage also occurred near Sleepy Eye and Hanska as the severe thunderstorms moved across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crops were damaged due to severe straight line winds around Sleepy Eye. In addition, tree branches were blown down in the town of Sleepy Eye, which blocked part of Highway 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed across South Dakota, moved into southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of Saturday, June 20th. There were several reports of large branches and a few trees blown down across Brown County. The town of Comfrey had 6-7 blocks that were closed temporarily due to tree debris across the road. Crop damage also occurred near Sleepy Eye and Hanska as the severe thunderstorms moved across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees and several large tree branches were blown down in the southwest part of Hanska. Crops were also damaged in parts of Lake Hanska Township. Power was out in Hanska for 4 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere combined with ample wind shear to result in the development of storms with large hail, severe winds, a tornado, and very heavy rainfall from Alexandria to Lake Mille Lacs...then southeast along Interstate 94. ||Initially, a severe thunderstorm developed near Fergus Falls during the afternoon of Sunday, July 12th.  As the afternoon progressed, several severe thunderstorms developed south toward Wheaton, then spread east-southeast toward Alexandria. One severe storm moved rapidly eastward across the Todd, Wadena county line and eventually produced a tornado and widespread straight-line wind damage. This tornado moved from Wadena County and into Todd County, destroying a barn south of Aldrich and causing numerous downed trees and power lines. The tornado was accompanied by damaging rear flank downdraft and downburst winds, and the same storm produced wind damage in northwest Morrison County. ||Severe storms moved into the area west of Alexandria and produced winds greater than 80 mph in some locations.  Near Brandon, there was structural damage to a large shed, a large pontoon boat was flipped on Maple Lake, and there were numerous downed trees and power lines.   ||Severe storms merged in west central and central Minnesota and then developed more into a line of storms and produced periodic downed trees, power lines, and occasional estimated winds of 60-70 mph as the storms moved southeast along Interstate 94 and into the Twin Cities metro area. Damage was not as widespread after it moved out of central Minnesota and into east central Minnesota, due to less wind shear and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down northeast of Garfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey revealed that a rear flank downdraft (RFD) accompanied a tornado, and produced tree damage to the south of the tornado track.  Numerous trees were tipped over and large branches were broken.  Once the tornado dissipated about two miles west of Staples, the RFD broadened out and swept across the south side of Staples, where it continued to topple dozens of trees. The last damage noted from the RFD was southeast of Staples. Several outbuildings were damaged, and trees fell across many vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Monday, June 22nd, a complex of thunderstorms (classified by some as a weak derecho event) developed across western South Dakota and moved rapidly eastward into southwest Minnesota before daybreak. Several bow-echo segments and a few isolated storms ahead of the main thunderstorm complex affected a large area along and south of the Minnesota River Valley, and in the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities between 4 am and 6 am LST. ||Initially, a line of thunderstorms that bowed out in far southern Minnesota, caused numerous downed trees, power lines, and some structural damage to outbuildings and homes as winds of 60 to 70 mph moved across the area between 4 am and 6 am LST.   ||There were isolated severe storms that developed ahead of the main line and moved northeast across south central and into the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities. Golf ball size hail was reported west of Lakeville before sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado mostly crossed corn and bean fields, but it did originate near a freshly cut alfalfa field. The alfalfa was easily blown and resulted in a debris signature on radar. The tornado produced very minor damage at a few farmsteads, and knocked down several dozen trees along with breaking many large branches. The tornado was also photographed by a local resident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some thunderstorms rolled through the U. S. Highway 2 corridor during the evening of the 11th and early morning hours of the 12th, conditions set up for a larger outbreak of severe weather on the afternoon of the 12th. Afternoon temperatures along the Red River Valley into portions of west central Minnesota topped out in the lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. A stationary boundary set up from northeast North Dakota into west central Minnesota and upper level winds were very favorable to sustain storms. Numerous thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and evening which produced large hail, tornadoes, and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters located two miles southwest of Doran viewed and photographed the tornado near highway 75 west-southwest of Doran. The tornado briefly touched down in a bean field. Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Saturday, June 6th, a front was moving slowly southeast across central and west central Minnesota. A line of showers and thunderstorms was developing along it as it sagged to the east-southeast. Between 9 and 10:30 PM LST, there was one band of very heavy rainfall, with rates of 1-2 inches per hour, that was moving across northern Stearns County. By midnight, almost all of northern Stearns County, between Sauk Centre and Sartell, and north of I-94, were 1.5 times above their 3 hour flash flood guidance. A small area between Melrose and Albany, and north of I-94, were 2 to 3 times above their 3 hour flash flood guidance. In addition, Dual Pol Doppler Radar estimated over 5 inches of rain fell between Melrose, St. Rosa, Holdingford and Freeport in a 3 hour period. A cooperative observer in Melrose reported 4.50 inches of rainfall that next morning. Local officials reported flooding southeast of Freeport where a county road was washed out. The town of Albany also had street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local officials in Stearns County reported flooding southeast of Freeport on County Road 172 and 157 after midnight.  A few cars and a party bus floated off the road into a ditch at that location. One person was injured when the party bus tipped over in the ditch. Part of the road was damaged due to flood waters flowing over that roadway. The town of Albany also had street flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and a wooden swing were blown down north of Waseca near County Road 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved across southeast Minnesota during the early morning of June 22nd. In the Austin (Mower County) area, over three inches of rain fell in about three hours. This rain overwhelmed the drainage systems in the city causing considerable urban flooding to occur. Interstate 90 was closed as parts of it became completely submerged in water and U.S. Highway 218 on the east side of the city also had water on it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rains caused Dobbins Creek in Austin to go out of its banks. The creek crested less than a half foot above the flood stage at 11.9 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Saturday, June 27th, a weak surface low moved southward across eastern North Dakota while a strong disturbance moved southward enhancing the instability and wind shear across western Minnesota. Several severe storms moved southward along the Minnesota, North Dakota border, and produced severe wind gusts, wind damage and large hail. The local emergency manager indicated that the majority of Lac Que Parle county was impacted east of Highway 75 and north of Highway 40, about 7-8 miles wide by 18 miles long.  Townships affected include Agassiz, Perry, Lake Shore North and South, Madison, Hantho and Cerro Gordo.  Wind damage was also reported in Bellingham, Dawson and Boyd.

Damage was not as widespread after it moved out of central Minnesota and into east central Minnesota, due to less wind shear and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved out of Wadena County (where it was rated EF2) and into Todd County.  A barn was collapsed just south of Aldrich, with roofing material blown 1/2 mile downwind. Dozens of trees were broken or uprooted in this area. Several wooden power poles were snapped. An outbuilding was also destroyed. Farther east, an outbuilding was destroyed along with a roof partially blown off near the intersection of 500th street and 223rd Avenue, just northwest of Dower Lake.  Significant tree damage was noted on the northeast side of Dower Lake, and concentrated damage continued in a path to about two miles west of Staples. Separate entries have also been made for numerous trees blown down due to the rear flank downdraft and downbursts north of the tornado track.

By the early evening of August 7th, a cold front had moved to a Hallock, Minnesota, to Valley City, North Dakota, line. Temperatures ahead of the front warmed into the low to mid 80s with dew point values in the middle 60s. Scattered thunderstorms formed along the boundary and moved east, producing mainly large hail, but some strong wind gusts also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to dime size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed across South Dakota, moved into southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of Saturday, June 20th. There were several reports of large branches and a few trees blown down across Brown County. The town of Comfrey had 6-7 blocks that were closed temporarily due to tree debris across the road. Crop damage also occurred near Sleepy Eye and Hanska as the severe thunderstorms moved across the county.

A thunderstorm complex that developed across South Dakota, moved into southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of Saturday, June 20th. There were several reports of large branches and a few trees blown down across Brown County. The town of Comfrey had 6-7 blocks that were closed temporarily due to tree debris across the road. Crop damage also occurred near Sleepy Eye and Hanska as the severe thunderstorms moved across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The town of Comfrey had 6-7 blocks of tree debris from severe straight line winds. Power was out for an hour around the community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 12th a mid level ridge and hot surface temperatures sat over the Rocky Mountains and high plains, with Minnesota lying on the eastern periphery. By mid afternoon, temperatures had risen into the mid to upper 90s across western and central North Dakota, and into the lower 90s along the Red River Valley. To the east in Minnesota, temperatures peaked in the 80s. Afternoon dew point values rose into the mid 60s to around 70. Thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon over the northern Red River Valley and tracked southeast through Wadena County through the early evening. Then, a second round of storms fired up over Kittson County shortly after the first round exited Wadena County. This second round of storms tracked nearly identically to first round, or from Kittson County southeast through Wadena County. These storms produced very large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous very large hailstones were collected and photographed, measuring from 4 to 4.5 inches in diameter.

After some thunderstorms rolled through the U. S. Highway 2 corridor during the evening of the 11th and early morning hours of the 12th, conditions set up for a larger outbreak of severe weather on the afternoon of the 12th. Afternoon temperatures along the Red River Valley into portions of west central Minnesota topped out in the lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. A stationary boundary set up from northeast North Dakota into west central Minnesota and upper level winds were very favorable to sustain storms. Numerous thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and evening which produced large hail, tornadoes, and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell across northwest Aastad Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow cover for early January 2015 was pretty minimal over most of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. South of the United States Highway 2 corridor, snow depths generally ranged around an inch or less. Along the Canadian border, amounts increased to 5 to 10 inches. A weak Alberta Clipper moved through the Northern Plains on the morning of January 8th, spreading a swath of light snow over the area. Snowfall amounts were generally light, or around an inch or so. By mid to late morning into the early afternoon, northwest winds increased, which resulted in blowing snow and reduced visibilities in open country. As winds gusted to 50 mph, whiteout conditions were reported in open areas mainly over northeast North Dakota into the northwest corner of Minnesota.

After some thunderstorms rolled through the U. S. Highway 2 corridor during the evening of the 11th and early morning hours of the 12th, conditions set up for a larger outbreak of severe weather on the afternoon of the 12th. Afternoon temperatures along the Red River Valley into portions of west central Minnesota topped out in the lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. A stationary boundary set up from northeast North Dakota into west central Minnesota and upper level winds were very favorable to sustain storms. Numerous thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and evening which produced large hail, tornadoes, and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous oak and pine trees were snapped and other large branches and limbs were blown down across southern Wadena Township.

Surface low pressure tracked from eastern Montana on the evening of January 2nd, into northeast South Dakota by the morning of January 3rd. This spread a swath of heavier snow from northeast North Dakota into northwest Minnesota, where four to ten inches of snow were reported north of the U. S. Highway 2 corridor. This was one of the first events of the winter season, which had been relatively quiet up to this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 12th a mid level ridge and hot surface temperatures sat over the Rocky Mountains and high plains, with Minnesota lying on the eastern periphery. By mid afternoon, temperatures had risen into the mid to upper 90s across western and central North Dakota, and into the lower 90s along the Red River Valley. To the east in Minnesota, temperatures peaked in the 80s. Afternoon dew point values rose into the mid 60s to around 70. Thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon over the northern Red River Valley and tracked southeast through Wadena County through the early evening. Then, a second round of storms fired up over Kittson County shortly after the first round exited Wadena County. This second round of storms tracked nearly identically to first round, or from Kittson County southeast through Wadena County. These storms produced very large hail and strong wind gusts.

August 15th was the fourth day of 90 plus degree temperatures across eastern North Dakota. A cold front over western North Dakota advanced into eastern North Dakota during the evening. Thunderstorms formed out ahead of the front in the Valley City, North Dakota area, and tracked east-southeast along Interstate 94 into the Fargo-Moorhead area before they briefly weakened. As the echoes worked into portions of Clay County, they intensified again. These storms continued tracking east-southeast through eastern Otter Tail County, producing strong wind gusts and large hail.

This was one of the first events of the winter season, which had been relatively quiet up to this point.

On August 12th a mid level ridge and hot surface temperatures sat over the Rocky Mountains and high plains, with Minnesota lying on the eastern periphery. By mid afternoon, temperatures had risen into the mid to upper 90s across western and central North Dakota, and into the lower 90s along the Red River Valley. To the east in Minnesota, temperatures peaked in the 80s. Afternoon dew point values rose into the mid 60s to around 70. Thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon over the northern Red River Valley and tracked southeast through Wadena County through the early evening. Then, a second round of storms fired up over Kittson County shortly after the first round exited Wadena County. This second round of storms tracked nearly identically to first round, or from Kittson County southeast through Wadena County. These storms produced very large hail and strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some thunderstorms rolled through the U. S. Highway 2 corridor during the evening of the 11th and early morning hours of the 12th, conditions set up for a larger outbreak of severe weather on the afternoon of the 12th. Afternoon temperatures along the Red River Valley into portions of west central Minnesota topped out in the lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. A stationary boundary set up from northeast North Dakota into west central Minnesota and upper level winds were very favorable to sustain storms. Numerous thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and evening which produced large hail, tornadoes, and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and gardens flattened.

Thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon over the northern Red River Valley and tracked southeast through Wadena County through the early evening. Then, a second round of storms fired up over Kittson County shortly after the first round exited Wadena County. This second round of storms tracked nearly identically to first round, or from Kittson County southeast through Wadena County. These storms produced very large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The funnel cloud was short-lived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some thunderstorms rolled through the U. S. Highway 2 corridor during the evening of the 11th and early morning hours of the 12th, conditions set up for a larger outbreak of severe weather on the afternoon of the 12th. Afternoon temperatures along the Red River Valley into portions of west central Minnesota topped out in the lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. A stationary boundary set up from northeast North Dakota into west central Minnesota and upper level winds were very favorable to sustain storms. Numerous thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and evening which produced large hail, tornadoes, and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread hail and wind damage occurred across central Oscar Township. Area wheat and corn fields were flattened and desiccated.

A strong clipper system sliding southeast across the region brought very strong northwest winds in behind it along with scattered snow showers. Snowfall amounts were less than an inch with the snow showers. The very strong northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph with gusts up to 60 mph combined with the existing snow cover and snow showers to bring blizzard conditions to west central Minnesota through the afternoon hours. Travel became difficult, if not, impossible.

A Canadian low pressure area moved through the Northern Plains into the Upper Midwest.  The low gradually slowed down and phased with a prominent low from the Southern Plains.  The low pressure area brought heavy snowfall of about 8 to nearly a foot to parts of far northern Minnesota, including Koochiching County and northern St. Louis County. International Falls had about 11.5, and a Kabetogama observer reported over 10 while Cook and Orr each recorded almost 8 inches of snowfall. .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident on U.S. Highway 63 north of Racine (Mower County). The accident occurred on icy roads created by occasional freezing rain that fell through the early morning hours of February 11th. The driver of a semi-truck lost control of his vehicle and struck a car, killing the driver of the car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The driver of a car was killed when the car was struck by a semi-truck. The driver of the semi-truck lost control of his vehicle on icy roads created by occasional freezing rain that had fallen during the early morning hours of February 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of arctic high pressure built across the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest Sunday, and early Monday, January 5th. Temperatures by Sunday evening fell into the teens below zero, and with wind speeds around 10 to 15 mph, created wind chill values between -30 to -40, especially in open areas along I-94 northwest of Sauk Centre. Winds decreased overnight, but temperatures held near -12 to -15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several surface observations in Todd County recorded temperatures around -10 to -15 degrees during the late evening and overnight hours. Wind speeds held around 10 to 15 mph, with occasional gusts near 20 mph, which created wind chill values between -30 to -40 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved southward across the Upper Midwest during the weekend of February 21st and 22nd. This air mass dropped actual temperatures into the teens below zero. As these temperatures combined with gusty northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph, wind chill values dropped below -35F for several hours. Some wind chill values reached near -45F around Alexandria, Glenwood, and Morris just after sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several local public and private weather stations indicated temperatures dropped to around -15F before sunrise. As winds remained around 10-15 mph, wind chill values dropped below -35F for several hours during the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota experienced blizzard conditions from the afternoon of January 8th into the very early morning hours of the 9th. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts as high as 50 mph, produced widespread whiteout conditions where the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less. The winds also caused severe drifting, making many roads impassable. The Minnesota Department of Transportation started recommending that travel was not advised in the area from the middle of the afternoon on the 8th into the overnight hours. The conditions were so bad in Mower County, that the sheriff issued an order that the only travel should be for emergency purposes. The whiteout conditions started occurring during the afternoon of the 8th ahead of an area of low pressure and cold front. Light snow fell ahead of the cold front, with 1 to 2 inches of accumulations, and was accompanied by south winds gusting to 25 to 30 mph. Once the cold front passed through, the winds switched to the northwest and increased to produce widespread whiteout conditions from the light snow that had fallen earlier in the day. These conditions then continued well past nightfall before the winds diminished during the early morning hours of the 9th. Most schools across the area dismissed early on the 8th and also had delayed starts on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained south winds of 15 to 25 mph, with higher gusts, produced sporadic whiteouts during the early afternoon of January 8th. Conditions deteriorated once a cold front moved through. The winds became northwest at 20 to 35 mph, with higher gusts, which produced widespread whiteouts from the 1 to 2 inches of snow that had fallen earlier in the day. The blowing and drifting snow created very poor driving conditions and all the schools in Dodge County dismissed early on the 8th and started late on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic cold front moved south across the Upper Midwest Tuesday, and dropped wind chill values around 35 degrees below zero by Wednesday morning, January 7. Numerous schools closed or were delayed a few hours due to dangerous cold wind chills that continued through the late morning. Although the winds continued to gust in the afternoon, temperatures rebounded into the single digits above zero, and lessened the overall dangerous wind chill values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local weather stations recorded wind speeds around 10 to 15 mph, with occasional gusts in excess of 25 mph during the morning of Wednesday, January 7th. As temperatures fell into the teens below zero, wind chill values fell to -35 degrees and continued for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds at Marshall in Lyon County in southwest Minnesota on the evening of June 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including trees blown over. The winds also damaged a billboard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds accompanying decreasing snowfall resulted in areas of blowing snow with visibility reduced to less than a mile in places after blizzard conditions abated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of arctic high pressure built across the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest Sunday, and early Monday, January 5th. Temperatures by Sunday evening fell into the teens below zero, and with wind speeds around 10 to 15 mph, created wind chill values between -30 to -40, especially in open areas along I-94 northwest of Sauk Centre. Winds decreased overnight, but temperatures held near -12 to -15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several surface observations in northwest Morrison County recorded temperatures around -10 to -15 degrees during the late evening and overnight hours. Wind speeds held around 10 mph, with occasional gusts near 15 mph, which created wind chill values around -35 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved southward across the Upper Midwest during the weekend of February 21st and 22nd. This air mass dropped actual temperatures into the teens below zero. As these temperatures combined with gusty northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph, wind chill values dropped below -35F for several hours. Some wind chill values reached near -45F around Alexandria, Glenwood, and Morris just after sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several local public and private weather stations indicated temperatures dropped to around -20F before sunrise. As winds remained around 10-15 mph, wind chill values dropped below -40F for several hours during the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across portions of southeast Minnesota on the morning of January 7th. The dangerous wind chills occurred behind an area of low pressure that moved across the central Mississippi River Valley on January 6th and as a strong area of high pressure dropped south out of Canada onto the High Plains. These two features combined to push air temperatures down into the single digits and teens below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 42 below from a mesonet reporting station near Grand Meadow (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Winona County on the morning of January 7th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 36 below from a mesonet reporting station near Ridgeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic cold front moved south across the Upper Midwest Tuesday, and dropped wind chill values around 35 degrees below zero by Wednesday morning, January 7. Numerous schools closed or were delayed a few hours due to dangerous cold wind chills that continued through the late morning. Although the winds continued to gust in the afternoon, temperatures rebounded into the single digits above zero, and lessened the overall dangerous wind chill values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local weather stations recorded wind speeds around 10 to 15 mph, with occasional gusts in excess of 25 mph during the morning of Wednesday, January 7th. As temperatures fell into the teens below zero, wind chill values fell to -35 degrees and continued for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Thursday, January 8th, snow fell across a wide area of Minnesota, with the heaviest in the eastern part of the state. Previous snowfall that occurred Monday, January 5th, plus a light dusting that occurred Thursday morning, combined with increasing northwest winds to produce blizzard conditions across a large area of western and southern Minnesota. ||By the early afternoon, winds started to gust over 50 mph in west central Minnesota. Area road weather cameras, plus the Minnesota Department of Transportation and local authorities, began to see numerous accidents develop. By 2 pm, several highways from west central Minnesota, southwest along the Minnesota River Valley, and along the Minnesota/Iowa border, closed due to severe blowing and drifting and car accidents. Conditions improved by the mid to late evening as winds slowly decreased and blowing snow lessened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region by the mid afternoon with winds gusting up to 50 mph in open country. Roads became hazardous with several roads closed during the height of the blizzard. Conditions started to improve during the evening, but roads remained very hazardous until after midnight as winds lessened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved southeast from the Dakotas into the central Mississippi River Valley from March 22nd into the 23rd. Heavy snow fell north of this system, with southeast Minnesota receiving between 6 and 10 inches. The snow fell at rates of 2 inches an hour at times, resulting in quick accumulations. Several accidents occurred as the roads quickly became snow covered. Numerous schools were either closed or had delayed starts on the 23rd. Moisture from the snow was responsible for a power pole catching file on the northwest side of Rochester (Olmsted County). The resulting power outage affected about 220 customers. The highest reported snowfall total was 10.6 inches south of Pine Island (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 8 inches of snow across Houston County. The highest reported total was 8 inches near Caledonia.

Precipitation amounts from the fall of 2014 going into the winter and spring of 2015 were below normal. For Fargo, ND, the precipitation total for September 2014 through March 2015 was 5.01 inches. Normal precipitation for this period is 9.16 inches. At Grand Forks, ND, the precipitation total for this period was 3.14 inches, while the normal was 7.94 inches. Therefore, precipitation totals at these two climate stations were running roughly about half of normal. A warm period at the beginning of March 2015 generally melted the little remaining snow cover left. Outside a few spots, there was no spring snowmelt flooding, either areal or in the river system. On the flip side, after the snow melted, there were a large number of fire weather concerns due to the dry fuels. Many Minnesota counties were placed in a D2 drought designation per the United States Drought Monitor.

A woman was found dead on a walking/bike trail about a quarter mile from where she was last seen walking three days earlier.  Temperatures around the time of her disappearance were in the teens below zero.  The official cause of death was hypothermia secondary to alcohol intoxication according to the county deputy sheriff/supervisor - Civil Process. The temperature was around 12 degrees F at the time she was last seen, then fell into the single digits below zero in the evening and then in the teens below zero overnight. The time of death is estimated.

During the morning of Thursday, January 8th, snow fell across a wide area of Minnesota, with the heaviest in the eastern part of the state. Previous snowfall that occurred Monday, January 5th, plus a light dusting that occurred Thursday morning, combined with increasing northwest winds to produce blizzard conditions across a large area of western and southern Minnesota. ||By the early afternoon, winds started to gust over 50 mph in west central Minnesota. Area road weather cameras, plus the Minnesota Department of Transportation and local authorities, began to see numerous accidents develop. By 2 pm, several highways from west central Minnesota, southwest along the Minnesota River Valley, and along the Minnesota/Iowa border, closed due to severe blowing and drifting and car accidents. Conditions improved by the mid to late evening as winds slowly decreased and blowing snow lessened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region by the mid afternoon with winds gusting up to 50 mph in open country. Roads became hazardous with several roads closed during the height of the blizzard. Conditions started to improve during the evening, but roads remained very hazardous until after midnight as winds lessened.

A weak upper level low pressure area moving over the region along with warm and moist air riding over subfreezing air at the surface brought freezing rain in the early and late morning hours.  The freezing rain resulted in ice accumulations of up to a tenth of inch  resulting in slippery roads and sidewalks. This resulted in slow travel,  a few accidents, along with late school starts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved southeast from the Dakotas into the central Mississippi River Valley from March 22nd into the 23rd. Heavy snow fell north of this system, with southeast Minnesota receiving between 6 and 10 inches. The snow fell at rates of 2 inches an hour at times, resulting in quick accumulations. Several accidents occurred as the roads quickly became snow covered. Numerous schools were either closed or had delayed starts on the 23rd. Moisture from the snow was responsible for a power pole catching file on the northwest side of Rochester (Olmsted County). The resulting power outage affected about 220 customers. The highest reported snowfall total was 10.6 inches south of Pine Island (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 8 inches of snow across Mower County. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches in Grand Meadow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, March 3rd, a band of snow developed across west central and into central Minnesota. Several inches of snow fell before 6 am from Morris, northeast to Alexandria, Long Prairie and Lake Mille Lacs. The snow tapered off Tuesday morning before 9 am, but gusty northwest winds developed as a strong cold front moved southward into western Minnesota by noon. Locally, 4 to 7 inches of snow fell from Morris to Glenwood, Alexandria, Long Prairie and Little Falls. The highest snowfall total was around 7 inches north of Alexandria. ||Localized blizzard conditions developed by the mid to late morning, before tapering off by early afternoon. Several Minnesota Department of Transportation web cams showed white out conditions, and local surface observations recorded sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts over 40 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began across Douglas County during the early morning hours of Tuesday, March 3rd. Moderate to heavy snow fell in a short period of time before sunrise, then tapered off to light snow and ended before 10 am. Locally 4-7 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across the region brought very strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph. These strong winds combined with 2 to 4 inches of newly fallen snow resulted in blizzard conditions during the mid and late morning hours. Visibilities were frequently below a quarter mile disrupting or halting travel across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms began to develop across northern Iowa during the early afternoon of Tuesday, April 7th. A low freezing level and elevated instability were ample enough to create a few large hail reports along the Minnesota, Iowa border. Most of the hail was one half inch or smaller, but a few did manage to drop quarter size hail near Fairmont and northwest of Albert Lea near Hartland.

Thunderstorms moved into southeast Minnesota during the early evening hours of May 3rd. One man was killed in Goodview (Winona County) when sub-severe thunderstorms winds uprooted a tree from the side of a bluff. The tree fell into the roadway, landing  on top of a pickup truck, crushing the passenger compartment and killing the man. Otherwise, the storms produced some large hail. The largest hail reported was walnut sized near Hayfield (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walnut sized hail fell east of Hayfield.

Northeast of this surface low, stronger forcing developed aloft across southern Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening. ||A band of moderate snow, with snowfall rates of one inch per hour, developed along the Minnesota River Valley during the mid afternoon and drifted northeast. This band set up up from near Hutchinson, east-southeast to Jordan, Northfield and Zumbrota between 4 PM LST to 9 PM LST. Snowfall rates during this time averaged between 1-2 inches per hour before the rates tapered off to 1/2 inch per hour after 9 PM LST.  ||By the time the snow ended early Monday morning, a 30 mile band of 6-12 inches of snow fell from Hutchinson, southeast to Jordan, Northfield and Zumbrota.||The following are some of the higher snowfall reports from this storm. ||12.3 inches near Zumbrota. |11.0 inches near Green Isle. |10.5 inches near Montgomery. |10.0 inches near Henderson. |9.8 inches near Lakeville. |9.5 inches near New Prague.|9.5 inches near Prior Lake. |9.0 inches near Brownton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of trained observers, social media pictures, and the general public estimated or measured between 7 and 12 inches of snow that fell between the late afternoon, and late evening of Sunday, March 22nd. The heaviest snow fell in the southern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms began to develop across northern Iowa during the early afternoon of Tuesday, April 7th. A low freezing level and elevated instability were ample enough to create a few large hail reports along the Minnesota, Iowa border. Most of the hail was one half inch or smaller, but a few did manage to drop quarter size hail near Fairmont and northwest of Albert Lea near Hartland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started near mile marker 88 on Highway 210 east of Aldrich in Bartlett Township. Two buildings were destroyed, one being a house. No one was injured in the fire. The area consumed by the fire was estimated to be three miles long and a mile wide in sections 13, 12 and 1 of Bartlett Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started near mile marker 88 on Highway 210 east of Aldrich in Bartlett Township around 5 PM LST, Saturday, April 11th. Two buildings were destroyed, one being a house. No one was injured in the fire. The area consumed by the fire was estimated to be three miles long and a mile wide in sections 13, 12 and 1 of Bartlett Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped large hail and brought strong winds to parts of northeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 7th.
Thunderstorms dropped large hail and brought strong winds to parts of northeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 7th.

By the afternoon of the 28th, a weak surface low was located over northeast North Dakota with a warm front extending out to the east. Temperatures rose into the mid to upper 70s with dew points in the low 60s. Showers and thunderstorms flared up along the Red River Valley into northwest Minnesota, producing large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm outflow produced a broad area of blowing dust and one or more brief gustnadoes along the outflow boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced nickel to quarter size hail in Rock and Jackson Counties in southwest Minnesota during the late morning and early afternoon of April 7th.

Thunderstorms moved into southeast Minnesota during the early evening hours of May 3rd. One man was killed in Goodview (Winona County) when sub-severe thunderstorms winds uprooted a tree from the side of a bluff. The tree fell into the roadway, landing  on top of a pickup truck, crushing the passenger compartment and killing the man. Otherwise, the storms produced some large hail. The largest hail reported was walnut sized near Hayfield (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walnut sized hail fell in Hayfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of -40 to -50 degrees Fahrenheit developed over northeast Minnesota late February 21st into the morning of February 22nd.

A southeast Minnesota home was damaged from a lightning strike during a late evening thunderstorm on April 8th. The Rochester (Olmsted County) home sustained about $2,500 in damage when lightning hit the natural gas line feeding the house starting a small fire that burned some siding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit the natural gas line to a home on the southwest side of Rochester. A small fire was started that burned some siding and soffit on the exterior of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts from the fall of 2014 going into the winter and spring of 2015 were below normal. For Fargo, ND, the precipitation total for September 2014 through March 2015 was 5.01 inches. Normal precipitation for this period is 9.16 inches. At Grand Forks, ND, the precipitation total for this period was 3.14 inches, while the normal was 7.94 inches. Therefore, precipitation totals at these two climate stations were running roughly about half of normal. A warm period at the beginning of March 2015 generally melted the little remaining snow cover left. Outside a few spots, there was no spring snowmelt flooding, either areal or in the river system. On the flip side, after the snow melted, there were a large number of fire weather concerns due to the dry fuels. Many Minnesota counties were placed in a D2 drought designation per the United States Drought Monitor.

Strong thunderstorms developed in far southern Minnesota during the late afternoon, and early evening of Monday, May 25th. A few strong storms moved from near Mankato, east-northeast toward Faribault before dissipating in southeast Minnesota. Penny size hail was reported in these storms as they tracked across the area.

By the afternoon of the 28th, a weak surface low was located over northeast North Dakota with a warm front extending out to the east. Temperatures rose into the mid to upper 70s with dew points in the low 60s. Showers and thunderstorms flared up along the Red River Valley into northwest Minnesota, producing large hail and strong wind gusts.

By the afternoon of the 28th, a weak surface low was located over northeast North Dakota with a warm front extending out to the east. Temperatures rose into the mid to upper 70s with dew points in the low 60s. Showers and thunderstorms flared up along the Red River Valley into northwest Minnesota, producing large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drifts of pea size hail were also reported.

Dry conditions continued from late April into early May, resulting a D2 drought designation on the U. S. Drought Monitor. Then a rapid switch from dry to wet occurred. From May 10th through the end of the month, consistent bouts of one to three inch rainfalls occurred. The nearby climate station at Fargo, North Dakota, ended up with a record precipitation total for May, with records dating back to the 1880s.

On August 12th a mid level ridge and hot surface temperatures sat over the Rocky Mountains and high plains, with Minnesota lying on the eastern periphery. By mid afternoon, temperatures had risen into the mid to upper 90s across western and central North Dakota, and into the lower 90s along the Red River Valley. To the east in Minnesota, temperatures peaked in the 80s. Afternoon dew point values rose into the mid 60s to around 70. Thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon over the northern Red River Valley and tracked southeast through Wadena County through the early evening. Then, a second round of storms fired up over Kittson County shortly after the first round exited Wadena County. This second round of storms tracked nearly identically to first round, or from Kittson County southeast through Wadena County. These storms produced very large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near the Pennington County line.

Precipitation amounts from the fall of 2014 going into the winter and spring of 2015 were below normal. For Fargo, ND, the precipitation total for September 2014 through March 2015 was 5.01 inches. Normal precipitation for this period is 9.16 inches. At Grand Forks, ND, the precipitation total for this period was 3.14 inches, while the normal was 7.94 inches. Therefore, precipitation totals at these two climate stations were running roughly about half of normal. A warm period at the beginning of March 2015 generally melted the little remaining snow cover left. Outside a few spots, there was no spring snowmelt flooding, either areal or in the river system. On the flip side, after the snow melted, there were a large number of fire weather concerns due to the dry fuels. Many Minnesota counties were placed in a D2 drought designation per the United States Drought Monitor.

Surface low pressure tracked from eastern Montana on the evening of January 2nd, into northeast South Dakota by the morning of January 3rd. This spread a swath of heavier snow from northeast North Dakota into northwest Minnesota, where four to ten inches of snow were reported north of the U. S. Highway 2 corridor. This was one of the first events of the winter season, which had been relatively quiet up to this point.

Thunderstorms developed across southeast Minnesota during the evening of May 7th. As these storms moved across the area, they produced some hail. The largest hail reported was dime sized northwest of Rochester (Olmsted County).

Strong thunderstorms developed in far southern Minnesota during the late afternoon, and early evening of Monday, May 25th. A few strong storms moved from near Mankato, east-northeast toward Faribault before dissipating in southeast Minnesota. Penny size hail was reported in these storms as they tracked across the area.

The afternoon of Thursday, August 6th, severe thunderstorms began to develop across northeast South Dakota and move east into west central Minnesota. Two thunderstorms, one near Elbow Lake, and another near Benson, moved eastward and caused damage near Evansville, and a brief tornado touchdown west of Sunburg. The Evansville storm weakened as it moved eastward into Todd County. The storm west of Sunburg, moved slowly eastward and produced several brief tornado touchdowns, mainly in open fields. However, the town of Hawick had some minor damage associated with the brief tornado that touchdown. Other storms produced damage near Canby as they moved from eastern South Dakota, into west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado briefly touched down in a corn field and was caught on video by multiple storm chasers.  It was also witnessed by fire department personnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first severe thunderstorms that developed across far northwest Minnesota during the early evening of Wednesday, August 12th, moved into northern Morrison County and produced baseball size hail in Camp Ripley. This storm moved southward into southern Morrison County and dissipated. Other storms that developed in northern Minnesota later the same night, moved into Todd and Douglas Counties and produced some hail and wind damage during the morning hours of Thursday, August 13th. These storms moved southward and weakened as they moved toward the Twin Cities metro area.||Scattered showers from decaying thunderstorms earlier across central Minnesota moved rapidly south across Isanti County, and brought brief wind gusts over 50 mph and caused several trees to blow down in the city of Cambridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball size hail fell in Camp Ripley during the evening of Wednesday, August 12th. The next morning, golf ball size hail was still in the ditches where the large hail fell the night before.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several boundaries set up across the Northern Plains during the evening of June 2nd and early morning of June 3rd. A cold front was located from Hallock, Minnesota, to just south of Devils Lake, North Dakota. A little further south, a weak area of surface low pressure was located west of Aberdeen, South Dakota, with a trough extending northward toward Jamestown, North Dakota, then east-northeast toward Waskish, Minnesota. These boundaries provided the focus for thunderstorm development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon temperatures topped out around 80 degrees with dew points in the mid to upper 50s. Along with a cold pool aloft and a weak short wave, thunderstorms fired during the afternoon of the 7th. The strongest storms fired up over western Kittson County and tracked south-southeast into northwest Polk County. Most of the severe reports were hail, although there was also a funnel cloud and several damaging wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent wall cloud and funnel tracked from just east of Alvarado into far northwest Polk County. No touchdown or associated damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Quite a bit of instability lingered across west central Minnesota on the afternoon of June 14th. However, cloud cover remained fairly extensive through the morning and early afternoon, which limited surface heating. However, enough heating did occur over Wadena County to generate one thunderstorm in the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A birch tree was blown down near Lyons State Forest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged in size from penny to quarter size. Pictures showed the hail thick enough to cover the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Thursday, August 6th, severe thunderstorms began to develop across northeast South Dakota and move east into west central Minnesota. Two thunderstorms, one near Elbow Lake, and another near Benson, moved eastward and caused damage near Evansville, and a brief tornado touchdown west of Sunburg. The Evansville storm weakened as it moved eastward into Todd County. The storm west of Sunburg, moved slowly eastward and produced several brief tornado touchdowns, mainly in open fields. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved across several farmsteads, destroying several outbuildings, uprooting or breaking hundreds of trees, and damaging crops.  About seven hay bales (each with a diameter of six feet) were blown apart for about 1/4 mile downwind. The damage pattern suggested this was a multiple vortex tornado.  The tornado was accompanied by a damaging rear flank downdraft just to the south of the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong rear flank downdraft winds accompanied a tornado.  The winds tipped over two silos that landed on top of a barn about 4 miles south of Canby. The winds also toppled dozens of trees. Even after the tornado dissipated, strong winds continued eastward into the city of Porter, with occasional tree and crop damage along the way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For most of northwest Minnesota, Sunday, October 11th, was a hot day with steady south winds. However, a cold front pushed through the Red River Valley by early evening. Just ahead of the front, winds took on a bit of a southwest to west direction, which is a good warming wind. Temperatures along the Red River Valley from Grand Forks southward surged into the 90s. Fargo hit 97 degrees, which was its warmest temperature of the year. Behind the front, temperatures would quickly fall with strong northwest winds. These strong winds caused several wildfires. Elsewhere, freshly plowed fields and the wind resulted in areas of blowing dust and low visibilities. These strong winds dropped off a little overnight, but quickly picked up again Monday morning. Hallock reported a peak wind of 58 mph.

Lightning damaged the bell tower at the St. Cloud Children's Home that was built in 1924. Although the main brick work wasn't damaged, the main damage occurred with the wooden structure surrounding the bell tower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike damaged a bell tower at the St. Cloud Children's Home that was built in 1924.
