EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail fell along with very heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms intensified as they moved into southern Polk County during the early morning hours of the 2nd. The strongest cells tracked from near Fertile, Minnesota, southeast into eastern Otter Tail County, where they produced very large hail.

On August 12th a mid level ridge and hot surface temperatures sat over the Rocky Mountains and high plains, with Minnesota lying on the eastern periphery. By mid afternoon, temperatures had risen into the mid to upper 90s across western and central North Dakota, and into the lower 90s along the Red River Valley. To the east in Minnesota, temperatures peaked in the 80s. Afternoon dew point values rose into the mid 60s to around 70. Thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon over the northern Red River Valley and tracked southeast through Wadena County through the early evening. Then, a second round of storms fired up over Kittson County shortly after the first round exited Wadena County. This second round of storms tracked nearly identically to first round, or from Kittson County southeast through Wadena County. These storms produced very large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A poplar tree was blown down and vehicles were dented by hail between Emmaville and Nevis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm hit southeast Minnesota on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into southeast Minnesota during the late morning of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates, causing a rapid deterioration in road conditions and dangerous travel conditions. By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across southeast Minnesota ranged from 5 to 8 inches. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).  Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported around 6 inches of snow across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches west of Hayfield. Winds gusting as high as 35 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon temperatures topped out around 80 degrees with dew points in the mid to upper 50s. Most of the severe reports were hail, although there was also a funnel cloud and several damaging wind reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms fired up during the afternoon along a cold front, which extended from Baudette to Park Rapids. One storm produced large hail in Hubbard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall along with intermittent large hail were reported near highway 71.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms flared up over portions of northwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of September 17th. One strong storm produced large hail south of Bagley.

A line of thunderstorms developed early Thursday morning, September 17th in eastern Minnesota, near the Twin Cities. These storms produced very heavy rainfall, gusty winds and some hail. The largest hail stone was quarter size near Crystal. Several thunderstorms moved across northern Dakota, southern Ramsey, and parts of western Washington Counties, and produced up to 2-3 inches of rainfall in less than 2 hours. These rainfall amounts and high rainfall rates, led to flash flooding along several highways during the morning commute in the southeast Twin Cities metro area. Several cars stalled in flood waters as they moved into areas of 2-3 feet of water along I-35E near St. Paul.

A line of showers and thunderstorms tracked from central North Dakota into portions of northwest Minnesota, with the strongest storms traveling along the highway 200 corridor. There were several bowing segments which produced strong winds and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked across southern portions of the city of Mahnomen, where it ripped roofing off of a maintenance shed, lifted and tipped a parked semi trailer, slid parked vehicles, and destroyed a small wooden shed. Flying debris impacted and damaged additional vehicles and structures near the casino. Peak wind speeds were estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms moved across southern Minnesota during the early morning of Tuesday, September 8th. Rainfall rates increased to around 1-2 inches per hour. Soil moisture was high across southern Minnesota from previous thunderstorms a day earlier. Therefore, more heavy rainfall in localized areas caused roads around Wells, Minnesota to become inundated with flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were flooded in the town of Wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed in northeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of Friday, July 17th. These storms moved slowly initially across northeast South Dakota where severe wind gusts, very large hail and a tornado occurred. ||After 730 PM CST, these severe storms began to bow out across west central Minnesota from Morris, southwest to Ortonville. Severe wind gusts were reported in Madison to Appleton City as the bow echo began to accelerate eastward across southern Minnesota.||Numerous reports of downed trees, power lines and occasional hail occurred from the South Dakota, Minnesota border eastward to the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin. ||Damage was more sporadic in western Minnesota, but became more widespread in eastern Minnesota as a more intense bow echo developed.  A widespread area of trees and power lines were blown down across the Twin Cities metro area, with some areas measuring wind gusts up to 61 knots. ||One of the bow echo segments produced a brief tornado west of Watertown, where a storm survey indicated an EF1 occurred.||Another significant wind damaged area was in Goodhue County where winds up to 85 mph were estimated based on a local survey. ||At one point during the storm, 225,000 people in Minnesota were without power according to Xcel Energy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on a power line in Brownton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm developed over Lake County in far northeast Minnesota on July 30th and dropped nickel-size hail.

On the evening of July 12th, 2015 a line of severe storms brought widespread damaging straight line winds to the southern portions of Cass and Crow Wing counties in north central Minnesota. There was major tree loss and damage to homes, cabins and vehicles from fallen trees. ||An automated weather station in Brainerd, MN recorded a peak wind of 65 mph during the storm. Most of the storm damage in the Brainerd area was consistent with winds in the 70 to 80 mph range.||However, there were pockets of more devastating tree damage, mainly in the Pillsbury State Forest area northwest of Brainerd. Wind speeds in excess of 100 mph were likely in this area. The Pillsbury State Forest was closed most of the summer due to trails roads being blocked by downed trees. By fall many trails and campsites were still closed. ||At area, 50 vehicles were destroyed 10,000 trees were blown down, 100 accommodation units destroyed and multiple conference centers and common buildings were damaged or destroyed. ||Lodges, resorts, golf courses  and campgrounds in the Gull Lake area were evacuated and closed for a week or more due to loss of power and for clean up operations. ||Cass and Crow Wing counties received state disaster assistance funds.||There as much at $25 million in damage, according to a rough tally among resorts owners, construction firms, utility providers and homeowners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 6 large healthy Oak trees down in the yard and many other trees down across the neighborhood. Power was out until 2:30 am. There was an estimated 2 of rainfall, but the gage was taken out by a falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures rose into the 80s during the afternoon of the 27th along with dew points in the low to mid 60s. By mid to late afternoon a cold front and a strong short wave moved into the region. At 3 p. m. the cold front extended along a line from Langdon to Lakota to just west of Jamestown. Showers and thunderstorms formed along the cold front, but the first strong thunderstorm formed in southern Manitoba and tracked south-southeast into the Pembina and Cavalier county areas. Most of the storms stayed west of the Red River, although a few did form to the east of it. In addition, most storms showed strong rotation in many different areas of each individual storm. These storms produced large hail and tornadoes. Local news media and storm chasers took many pictures during the event, as most of the tornadoes were visible from long distances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent wall cloud with funnel were observed over Pelican and Crystal lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed in northeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of Friday, July 17th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several complexes of thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and North Dakota, moved east-southeast into central Minnesota after midnight, and then southeast across southern Minnesota, mainly southeast of the Twin Cities metro area, during the early morning of Friday, July 24th. A few thunderstorms caused brief downbursts. A few gusts of 30-45 mph were measured by area surface observations, although two areas had damage associated with stronger winds with brief bowing out of the main thunderstorm complex. ||In addition to the brief severe wind gusts, torrential rainfall occurred where thunderstorms trained. A few areas received between two to four inches from southeast of Alexandria, to the west of the Twin Cities, south to the Iowa border. Isolated pockets of four to five inches occurred just west of the Twin Cities, where Howard Lake and Cokato both received over four inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Cleveland. The trees landed on homes, sheds and a car and caused substantial damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain near Woodstock in Pipestone County of southwest Minnesota shortly after midnight on the morning of July 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was measured at 4.76 inches. No flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, July 28th, a line of strong thunderstorms moved across eastern South Dakota, and into west central Minnesota. Most of the storms were non-severe and produced occasional wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. One cell moved near the city of Montevideo and blew down several trees at the Lac Qui Parle State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lac Qui Parle state park officials reported trees and large branches toppled, including some falling on a building. The building was barely damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of Sunday, July 5th, a line of thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary from west central Minnesota, northeast to the arrowhead region. These storms slowly moved southeast across west central and central Minnesota by midnight. By 2 to 3 am CST, numerous thunderstorms began to develop in east central Minnesota, ahead of the main line. ||As additional thunderstorms congealed with the main line, deep moisture continued to surge into southern Minnesota, with the effect of high precipitable water values, rainfall rates increased to 2-3 inches per hour. These storms trained and redeveloped in the southern and southeastern Twin Cities metro area, and moved eastward into west central Wisconsin. Several hours of moderate to heavy rainfall occurred from northern Dakota and southern Washington Counties, eastward into Wisconsin during the early morning hours of Monday, July 6th.||Storm totals ranged from 4-5 inches in northern Dakota County and southern Washington County.  Heavy rains overflowed a pond, and caused flash flooding at Highlands Park in Cottage Grove.  ||The following are some of the higher rainfall totals from Monday morning, July 6th.||Burnsville 4.73 inches. |Farmington 4.60 inches.|Cottage Grove 4.40 inches. |West St. Paul 4.25 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals exceeded 4-5 inches in a 3 hour period early Monday morning, July 6th.  A resident in Woodbury reported rising water approaching their home. Heavy rains overflowed a pond and the athletic area at Highlands Park in Cottage Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm hit southeast Minnesota on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into southeast Minnesota during the late morning of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates, causing a rapid deterioration in road conditions and dangerous travel conditions. By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across southeast Minnesota ranged from 5 to 8 inches. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).  Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 6 and 8 inches of snow across Winona County. The highest reported total was 8.3 inches south of the city of Winona. Winds gusting into the 30 to 35 mph range created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

Several severe thunderstorms developed in northeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of Friday, July 17th. These storms moved slowly initially across northeast South Dakota where severe wind gusts, very large hail and a tornado occurred. ||After 730 PM CST, these severe storms began to bow out across west central Minnesota from Morris, southwest to Ortonville. Severe wind gusts were reported in Madison to Appleton City as the bow echo began to accelerate eastward across southern Minnesota.||Numerous reports of downed trees, power lines and occasional hail occurred from the South Dakota, Minnesota border eastward to the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin. ||Damage was more sporadic in western Minnesota, but became more widespread in eastern Minnesota as a more intense bow echo developed.  A widespread area of trees and power lines were blown down across the Twin Cities metro area, with some areas measuring wind gusts up to 61 knots. ||One of the bow echo segments produced a brief tornado west of Watertown, where a storm survey indicated an EF1 occurred.||Another significant wind damaged area was in Goodhue County where winds up to 85 mph were estimated based on a local survey. ||At one point during the storm, 225,000 people in Minnesota were without power according to Xcel Energy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey indicated that an EF-0 tornado touch downed in the northwest portion of North Oaks, near Charley Lake, and moved east-northeast to the southern side of Deep Lake, where the greatest number of trees were toppled and snapped. Some of the trees fell on houses, vehicles and other structures. The tornado had a path length of 1.9 miles with maximum winds of 80 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey indicated a significant downburst caused damage west of Goodhue. Severe downburst winds initially caused damage to a barn and outbuildings at a farm about 9 miles west of Goodhue. Farther east, about 7.5 miles west of Goodhue, there were numerous trees blown down, an empty grain bin was destroyed, tin was blown off some outbuildings, doors were blown off a machine shed, and some corn was destroyed. These were the two hardest hit locations. Between the two locations, there were just a few trees or large branches taken down. Both locations exhibited divergent damage patterns indicative of a downburst. Based on the survey damage, the highest winds were around 80 to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm hit southeast Minnesota on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into southeast Minnesota during the late morning of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates, causing a rapid deterioration in road conditions and dangerous travel conditions. By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across southeast Minnesota ranged from 5 to 8 inches. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).  Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 4 and 6 inches of snow across Wabasha County. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches in Reads Landing. Winds gusting into the 30 to 35 mph range created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house east of Pine River, MN the morning on July 28th, causing substantial damage to the house. The home owner's insurance company estimated at least $155,000 in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house east of Pine River, MN the morning of July 28th. The lightning caused extensive damage.  It hit three large white pines and the house.  It scorched outlets and wires, and blew out sheet rock and light fixtures.  There was also some fire damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some thunderstorms rolled through the U. S. Highway 2 corridor during the evening of the 11th and early morning hours of the 12th, conditions set up for a larger outbreak of severe weather on the afternoon of the 12th. Afternoon temperatures along the Red River Valley into portions of west central Minnesota topped out in the lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. A stationary boundary set up from northeast North Dakota into west central Minnesota and upper level winds were very favorable to sustain storms. Numerous thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and evening which produced large hail, tornadoes, and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tracked for about two miles along and north of county road 16. The tornado snapped numerous pine trees and broke down large oak branches and limbs along its path. Peak winds were estimated at 90 mph. The tornado was viewed and photographed by spotters near Vining.

Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported around 5 inches of snow across Mower County. The highest reported total was 5 inches in Austin. Winds gusting to around 30 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 12th, 2015 a line of severe storms brought widespread damaging straight line winds to the southern portions of Cass and Crow Wing counties in north central Minnesota. There was major tree loss and damage to homes, cabins and vehicles from fallen trees. ||An automated weather station in Brainerd, MN recorded a peak wind of 65 mph during the storm. Most of the storm damage in the Brainerd area was consistent with winds in the 70 to 80 mph range.||However, there were pockets of more devastating tree damage, mainly in the Pillsbury State Forest area northwest of Brainerd. Wind speeds in excess of 100 mph were likely in this area. The Pillsbury State Forest was closed most of the summer due to trails roads being blocked by downed trees. By fall many trails and campsites were still closed. ||At area, 50 vehicles were destroyed 10,000 trees were blown down, 100 accommodation units destroyed and multiple conference centers and common buildings were damaged or destroyed. ||Lodges, resorts, golf courses  and campgrounds in the Gull Lake area were evacuated and closed for a week or more due to loss of power and for clean up operations. ||Cass and Crow Wing counties received state disaster assistance funds.||There as much at $25 million in damage, according to a rough tally among resorts owners, construction firms, utility providers and homeowners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell at Grand National Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms along a warm front brought large hail up to baseball size and damaging winds to parts of west central Minnesota.
Severe thunderstorms along a warm front brought large hail up to baseball size and damaging winds to parts of west central Minnesota.

After some thunderstorms rolled through the U. S. Highway 2 corridor during the evening of the 11th and early morning hours of the 12th, conditions set up for a larger outbreak of severe weather on the afternoon of the 12th. Afternoon temperatures along the Red River Valley into portions of west central Minnesota topped out in the lower 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s. A stationary boundary set up from northeast North Dakota into west central Minnesota and upper level winds were very favorable to sustain storms. Numerous thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and evening which produced large hail, tornadoes, and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado wrapped in heavy rain and damaging downburst winds tracked across southern Wadena and Aldrich townships. The tornado tore down four sets of H-pole high voltage power poles and demolished outbuildings and a storage barn at the England Prairie Pioneer Farms. The tornado also demolished a silo and ripped through sections of center pivot irrigation systems at multiple locations. Peak winds were estimated at 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed in northeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of Friday, July 17th. These storms moved slowly initially across northeast South Dakota where severe wind gusts, very large hail and a tornado occurred. ||After 730 PM CST, these severe storms began to bow out across west central Minnesota from Morris, southwest to Ortonville. Severe wind gusts were reported in Madison to Appleton City as the bow echo began to accelerate eastward across southern Minnesota.||Numerous reports of downed trees, power lines and occasional hail occurred from the South Dakota, Minnesota border eastward to the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin. ||Damage was more sporadic in western Minnesota, but became more widespread in eastern Minnesota as a more intense bow echo developed.  A widespread area of trees and power lines were blown down across the Twin Cities metro area, with some areas measuring wind gusts up to 61 knots. ||One of the bow echo segments produced a brief tornado west of Watertown, where a storm survey indicated an EF1 occurred.||Another significant wind damaged area was in Goodhue County where winds up to 85 mph were estimated based on a local survey. ||At one point during the storm, 225,000 people in Minnesota were without power according to Xcel Energy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey indicated that an EF-1 tornado touched down in Hollywood Township in Carver County. The tornado spun up seven miles west-southwest of Watertown and continued east-northeast to two miles west-southwest of Watertown. The most damage occurred as the tornado crossed Vega Avenue where numerous trees were snapped and some minor structural damage was found. An apple orchard was also damaged with this tornado. There was a private weather station that measured a 99 mph wind gust before it blew off the roof. The path length was 5.1 miles long with a maximum wind speed of 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of July 18th. One of these storms produced a small, short-lived tornado southeast of Theilman (Wabasha County). The tornado moved through a corn field and across one farmstead removing the tops off of two silos, blowing in garage doors and moved several tires off a sileage pile. Besides the tornado, the storms also produced damaging winds that blew down trees in Millville (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small, short-lived tornado damaged some corn, removed the tops off of two silos, blew in garage doors and moved tires off a sileage pile.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of July 18th. One of these storms produced a small, short-lived tornado southeast of Theilman (Wabasha County). The tornado moved through a corn field and across one farmstead removing the tops off of two silos, blowing in garage doors and moved several tires off a sileage pile. Besides the tornado, the storms also produced damaging winds that blew down trees in Millville (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along U.S. Highway 61 between Lake City and the city of Wabasha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rain, moved across portions of southeast Minnesota during the morning of July 24th. The storms dropped between 2 and 2.5 inches of rain on the Austin area (Mower County) in a short amount of time. This caused some street flooding to occur including Interstate 90 near 11th Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rains caused street flooding to occur in the Austin area, including Interstate 90 near 11th Street. Rainfall reports indicated between 2 and 2.5 inches of rain fell in a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the evening hours of August 6th. A lightning strike from one of these storms hit a tree and then traveled along a gas line into a rural Mazeppa (Wabasha County) home. A small fire inside the home was quickly extinguished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit a tree and then traveled along a gas line into a rural Mazeppa home. A small fire was started inside the house that was quickly extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted on the southwest side of Rochester (Olmsted County) during the late afternoon of August 9th. This funnel cloud developed as isolated showers and thunderstorms moved across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted on the southwest side of Rochester near the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed in northeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of Friday, July 17th. These storms moved slowly initially across northeast South Dakota where severe wind gusts, very large hail and a tornado occurred. ||After 730 PM CST, these severe storms began to bow out across west central Minnesota from Morris, southwest to Ortonville. Severe wind gusts were reported in Madison to Appleton City as the bow echo began to accelerate eastward across southern Minnesota.||Numerous reports of downed trees, power lines and occasional hail occurred from the South Dakota, Minnesota border eastward to the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin. ||Damage was more sporadic in western Minnesota, but became more widespread in eastern Minnesota as a more intense bow echo developed.  A widespread area of trees and power lines were blown down across the Twin Cities metro area, with some areas measuring wind gusts up to 61 knots. ||One of the bow echo segments produced a brief tornado west of Watertown, where a storm survey indicated an EF1 occurred.||Another significant wind damaged area was in Goodhue County where winds up to 85 mph were estimated based on a local survey. ||At one point during the storm, 225,000 people in Minnesota were without power according to Xcel Energy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe squall line that moved out of Carver County, and into Hennepin County produced significant tree, power pole and power line damage. Reports of numerous large trees were toppled or uprooted from the Hennepin, Carver County line, near Saint Bonifacius, to 4 miles northeast of Minnetrista, then east-northeast toward Maple Plain, Spring Lake and Orono. Numerous trees had diameters of over 12 inches. One of the hardest hit areas was near Saint Bonifacius, where a barn and silo were damaged. The Spirit of the Lakes Festival had several tents blown down, with lots of reports of wires and sparking.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused dropped large hail and caused damaging winds across parts of northeast Minnesota. The reports included a report of a 2.5 diameter hailstone near Bowstring, MN in Itasca County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The power lines fell down onto the deck and the power went out.

Severe wind gusts were reported in Madison to Appleton City as the bow echo began to accelerate eastward across southern Minnesota.||Numerous reports of downed trees, power lines and occasional hail occurred from the South Dakota, Minnesota border eastward to the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin. ||Damage was more sporadic in western Minnesota, but became more widespread in eastern Minnesota as a more intense bow echo developed.  A widespread area of trees and power lines were blown down across the Twin Cities metro area, with some areas measuring wind gusts up to 61 knots. ||One of the bow echo segments produced a brief tornado west of Watertown, where a storm survey indicated an EF1 occurred.||Another significant wind damaged area was in Goodhue County where winds up to 85 mph were estimated based on a local survey. ||At one point during the storm, 225,000 people in Minnesota were without power according to Xcel Energy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large bow echo surged across eastern Hennepin County causing numerous downed trees and power lines. Some of the trees were 12 inches in diameter. There were also several measured reports of severe wind gusts from private weather stations in Hennepin County. One measured wind gust was 58 knots. In addition, the Spirit of the Lakes Festival had shelters and tents blown down. The Spring Park City Hall had a tree blown down. At one point, 33,000 Xcel Energy customers were out of power in Hennepin County.

Most of the storm damage in the Brainerd area was consistent with winds in the 70 to 80 mph range.||However, there were pockets of more devastating tree damage, mainly in the Pillsbury State Forest area northwest of Brainerd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 9 inches followed a period of freezing drizzle over southwest Minnesota. The freezing drizzle fell after midnight on November 30th, then the accumulating snow developed that morning and continued through the morning of December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle was quickly followed by snow. The snow accumulated 5 to 9 inches over a 24 to 30 hour period, including 9.0 inches near Pipestone. The storm resulted in cancellations or cutbacks of school classes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area moving slowly across the region brought heavy snow of 6 to 7 inches to west central Minnesota. The snow began on November 30th and continued into December 1st.  Travel was difficult on the roads with travel not advised on many roads. Schools and events were also postponed or cancelled. Snowfall amounts include; 6 inches at Ortonville and Browns Valley; 7 inches at Artichoke, north or Cornell, and Odessa. See December storm data for the end of the storm.

The most snow, around a foot, fell in a narrower band from Jamestown to Michigan to Cavalier, in North Dakota. Outside this band, a broader four to six inches of snow fell along with breezy winds and a little mixed precipitation.

An area of surface low pressure over western South Dakota on the morning of December 22nd, moved northeast to near Aberdeen, South Dakota, by early evening. This low then progressed into southeast Manitoba, Canada, by the morning of December 23rd. Snowfall was enhanced by an upper level disturbance, which pivoted over northeast North Dakota and extreme northwest Minnesota. Three to five inches of snow fell over portions of the northwest corner of Minnesota, along with breezy winds and some light mixed precipitation.

One person was killed in a two car accident that occurred on icy roads south of Elgin (Olmsted County). The automated weather observing system at the Rochester (Olmsted County) Airport was reporting freezing rain at the time of the accident with temperatures in the lower 30s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two car accident on State Highway 42 south of Elgin. The roads were icy at the time of the accident and the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport was reporting freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very abnormally mild storm system for the middle of December tracked northeast across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest late Tuesday night and into Wednesday morning, December 16th. Due to the very mild air, most of the precipitation was in the form of rain, sleet and snow across southern Minnesota. In the far west, cooler air aloft allowed for snow to fall, with some areas reporting thunder snow in southwest and west central Minnesota during the height of this event. Both Canby and Madison AWOS reporting stations had measured 1/2 to 1 SM surface visibility between 3 and 8 am Wednesday morning. Local observers between 6-7 am reported 6 to 7 inches of snowfall around Canby and Madison, with final totals before noon around 7.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local observer in Canby reported 7.8 inches of snow that fell early Wednesday morning, December 16th. Snowfall rates during the height of the storm were between one and two inches per hour, especially when thunder was reported at the Canby airport around 2 and 3 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system began to organize across the Central Plains the evening of December 25th, and moved northeast across the Northern Plains, and Upper Midwest overnight and Friday morning, December 26th.  Snow developed across southern Minnesota toward midnight, and moved northward, producing a swath of 6 to locally 8 inches in southwest and portions of west central Minnesota before tapering off Friday morning. Some lingering light snow occurred in the late morning and afternoon of Friday, December 26th, but only an additional one inch of snow fell. The heaviest occurred in the far southwest part of Minnesota, but snowfall amounts reached 6 to 8 inches near Redwood Falls, north and northeast toward Olivia. Also, up to 7 inches fell near Canby in far western Yellow Medicine County.  Further to the east and north, snowfall amounts tapered off to less than 6 inches, with most areas receiving between 2 and 5 inches around Hutchinson, St. Cloud, the Twin Cities metro area, and Little Falls toward Mora. Less than two inches fell in south central Minnesota and most of west central Wisconsin, where some sleet and freezing drizzle was reported before snow developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports of 6 to 8  inches of snow fell near Redwood Falls. The snow tapered off by late morning, but the bulk of the heavier snowfall occurred between 10 pm to 6 am LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 10 inches in part of southwest Minnesota over an 18 to 24 hour period from December 28th into the morning of December 29th. The heavy snow fell in an area near Interstate 90 and north to the Windom area. Accumulations dropped off sharply to the northwest with less than one inch in Lincoln and northern Lyon Counties. Winds were light to moderate with no blowing snow reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 6 to 9 inches, including 8.5 inches at Lismore. Winds were light to moderate with no blowing snow reported.

Heavy snow which began on November 30th wound down across west central Minnesota on December 1st as a large upper level low pressure area moved slowly across the region.  Heavy snowfall of 6 to 7 inches fell across west central Minnesota.  Travel was difficult on the roads with travel not advised on many roads. Schools and events were also postponed or cancelled. Snowfall amounts include: 6 inches at Ortonville and Browns Valley; and 7 inches at Artichoke Lake, north of Cornell, and Odessa.

A surface low pressure area which moved from eastern Colorado to Minnesota brought heavy snow to Big Stone County. Northwest winds also increased to 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 40 mph. This caused blowing snow and lowered visibility. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 7 inches occurred with this storm. Schools were delayed or cancelled with travel not advised.  Snowfall amounts include 6 inches at Ortonville and Artichoke Lake and 7 inches at Odessa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very abnormally mild storm system for the middle of December tracked northeast across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest late Tuesday night and into Wednesday morning, December 16th. Due to the very mild air, most of the precipitation was in the form of rain, sleet and snow across southern Minnesota. In the far west, cooler air aloft allowed for snow to fall, with some areas reporting thunder snow in southwest and west central Minnesota during the height of this event. Both Canby and Madison AWOS reporting stations had measured 1/2 to 1 SM surface visibility between 3 and 8 am Wednesday morning. Local observers between 6-7 am reported 6 to 7 inches of snowfall around Canby and Madison, with final totals before noon around 7.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the local media and trained observers reported 7.5 inches of snow that fell early Wednesday morning, December 16th. Snowfall rates during the height of the storm were between one and two inches per hour, especially when thunder was reported at the Madison airport around 2 and 3 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 10 inches in part of southwest Minnesota over an 18 to 24 hour period from December 28th into the morning of December 29th. The heavy snow fell in an area near Interstate 90 and north to the Windom area. Accumulations dropped off sharply to the northwest with less than one inch in Lincoln and northern Lyon Counties. Winds were light to moderate with no blowing snow reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 6 to 9 inches, including 9.0 inches near Jackson. Winds were light to moderate with no blowing snow reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon temperatures topped out around 80 degrees with dew points in the mid to upper 50s. Along with a cold pool aloft and a weak short wave, thunderstorms fired during the afternoon of the 7th. The strongest storms fired up over western Kittson County and tracked south-southeast into northwest Polk County. Most of the severe reports were hail, although there was also a funnel cloud and several damaging wind reports.

Temperatures rose into the 80s during the afternoon of the 27th along with dew points in the low to mid 60s. By mid to late afternoon a cold front and a strong short wave moved into the region. At 3 p. m. the cold front extended along a line from Langdon to Lakota to just west of Jamestown. Showers and thunderstorms formed along the cold front, but the first strong thunderstorm formed in southern Manitoba and tracked south-southeast into the Pembina and Cavalier county areas. Most of the storms stayed west of the Red River, although a few did form to the east of it. In addition, most storms showed strong rotation in many different areas of each individual storm. These storms produced large hail and tornadoes. Local news media and storm chasers took many pictures during the event, as most of the tornadoes were visible from long distances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was observed from Interstate 94, where a series of dust plumes were noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm hit southeast Minnesota on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into southeast Minnesota during the late morning of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates, causing a rapid deterioration in road conditions and dangerous travel conditions. By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across southeast Minnesota ranged from 5 to 8 inches. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).  Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 5 and 7 inches of snow across Fillmore County. The highest reported total was 7.1 inches at Wykoff. Winds gusting as high as 35 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on Saturday, August 8, 2015 over north-central Minnesota. A cool front moved eastward across northern Minnesota during the afternoon and evening hours and interacted with a northward moving outflow boundary. Storms initiated over west-central and central Minnesota near the intersection of the cool front and the outflow boundary and moved generally east-southeastward through late afternoon and early evening. Several additional thunderstorms developed along the cool front over northern and central St. Louis County and progressed east to southeastward. The strongest storms occurred between 3 PM CDT and 7 PM CDT.  One supercell developed a tornado near Moilan Lake, near Makinen, MN, in the late afternoon.  The tornado was rated EF-1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed east of Zim, MN at 3:40 PM CDT and moved initially northeast, but quickly turned easterly, taking on supercell characteristics by 4:00 PM CDT. A tornado developed at 4:25 PM CDT one-quarter mile northwest of Moilan Lake and uprooted several trees on the northwest shore and continuing to the northeast shore. Damage at the start of the track was rated EF-0 with estimated peak winds of 80 mph. The tornado initially tracked southeastward and turned easterly over a homestead on the southeast shore of the lake. Numerous mature softwood trees were uprooted and some trees were broken near the base. Winds near the homestead were estimated between 85 and 90 mph, with the intensity increasing to low-end EF-1. The tornado continued to break and uproot mature trees as it traveled eastward and crossed Moilan Road. The tornado reached peak intensity as it traveled through a stand of trees one-quarter miles east of Moilan Road. Numerous trees were broken near their base. Winds at this location were estimated between 90 and 95 mph, still an EF-1 tornado. The tornado continued eastward and began to weaken crossing Tavern Road and turning northeast. Several trees were broken and uprooted near Tavern Road with estimated winds between 80 and 95 mph. The tornado moved across two homesteads east of Tavern Road as it was weakening and dissipated at 4:31 PM CDT after crossing Pond Road, approximately three-tenths of a mile east of the intersection with Tavern Road.
